                                        U.S. DEPARTMENT OF LABOR
                                                 Ray Marshall, Secretary


                                            BUREAU OF LABOR STATISTICS
                                              Julius Shiskin, Commissioner



                                        Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
                                    Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
                                    Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
                                    ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
                                    Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
                                    of the Census (Department of Commerce), Employ-
                                    ment and Training Administration, State Employ-
                                    ment Security Agencies, and State Departments of
                                    Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of Labor
                                    Statistics. A brief description of the cooperative
                                    statistical programs of the BLS with these agencies
                                    is presented in the Explanatory Notes. The State
                                    agencies are listed on the inside back cover.
                                         Employment and Earnings may be ordered
                                    through the Superintendent of Documents, U.S.
                                    Government Printing Office, Washington, D.C.
                                    20402. Subscription price per year $18 domes-
                                    tic, and $22.50 foreign. Single copy $1.50 domes-
                                    tic,and $1.90 foreign. Prices are subject to change
                                    by the U.S. Government Printing Office. Send
                                    correspondence on circulation and subscription
                                    matters (including address changes) to the Super-
                                    intendent of Documents.

                                        Communications on editorial matters should be
                                    addressed to: Editors, Employment and Earnings,
                                    Bureau of Labor Statistics, Washington, D.C. 20212.
                                    Inquiries regarding the text and Household Data
                                    should be addressed to: Attention of Gloria P.
                                    Green or Phone: (202) 523-1944. Inquiries relating
                                    to Establishment Data and all other tables should be'
                                    addressed to: Attention of Chester L. Green or
                                    Phone: (202) 523-1759.

                                        The Secretary of Labor has determined that
                                    the publication of this periodical is necessary in
                                    the transaction of the public business required by
                                    law of this Department. Use of funds for printing
                                    this periodical has been approved by the Director
                                    of the Office of Management and Budget through
                                    February 28, 1981. Controlled circulation postage
                                    paid at Washington, D.C. and Baltimore, Md.

                                    Library of Congress Catalog Number 70-11379

                                        Unless identified as copyrighted, articles and
                                    tabulations in this publication are in the public
                                    domain and may be reproduced without permis-
                                    sion of the Federal Government. Please credit Em-
                                    ployment and Earnings, Bureau of Labor Statistics.

                                              In addition to the monthly data appearing regularly in Employment and
                                              Earnings, special features appear in most of the issues, as shown below:
                                                                             Jan.   Feb.   Mar.   Apr.   May   July      Aug.   Oct.    Nov.   Dec.
                         Household data
                       Annual averages                                          X
                       Revised seasonally adjusted series                             X

                            The issue that introduces new benchmarks varies. The October 1975 issue marks the introduction of March 1974
                      benchmarks. Interim revisions of selected series appear in the February 1977 issue.

                            Revised data introduced December 1976 and February 1977.

             C-5. Gross and spendable average weekly earnings of production or nonsupervisory workers

                                                               Explanatory Notes

                             These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                             (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                             lished in Employment and Earnings.




                                                                      Introduction
               The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two            pling variability and response errors are additional reasons for
             major sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from        discrepancies. The major factors which have a differential
             employers,                                                           effect on levels and trends of the two series are as follows.
                 Data based on household interviews are obtained from a
                                                                                   Employment
             sample survey of the population 16 years of age and over. The
             survey is conducted each month by the Bureau of the Census            Coverage. The household survey definition of employment
             for the Bureau of Labor Statistics and provides comprehensive         comprises wage and salary workers (including domestics and
             data on the labor force, the employed and the unemployed,             other private household workers), self-employed persons, and
             including such characteristics as age, sex, race, household           unpaid workers who worked 15 hours or more during the sur-
             relationship, marital status, occupation, and industry attach-        vey week in family-operated enterprises. Employment in both
             ment. The survey also provides data on the characteristics and        agricultural and nonagricultural industries is included. The
             past work experience of thos.e not in the labor force. The infor-     payroll survey covers only wage and salary employees on the
             mation is collected by trained interviewers from a sample of          payrolls of nonagricultural establishments.
             about 47,000 households, representing 461 areas in 923
             counties and independent cities, with coverage in 50 States
                                                                                   Multiple jobholding. The household approach provides infor-
             and the District of Columbia. The data collected are based on         mation on the work status of the population without duplication,
             the activity or status reported for the calendar week including       since each person is classified as employed, unemployed, or
             the 12th of the month.                                                not in the labor force. Employed persons holding more than
                                                                                   one job are counted only once and are classified according to

                                                                         Establishment data
                                                                          (B, C, and D tables)


             COLLECTION                                                                         State agencies mail the forms to the establishments and
                                                                                             examine the returns for consistency, accuracy, and complete-
                Payroll reports provide current information on wage and                      ness. The States use the information to prepare State and area
             salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover in non-                  series and then send the establishment data to the BLS for use
             agricultural establishments, by industry and geographic location.               in preparing the national series.


             Federal-State cooperation                                                       Shuttle schedules

                 Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies, the                        Two types of data collection schedules are used: Form BLS
             respondent fills out a single employment or labor turnover                     790—Monthly Report on Employment, Payroll, and Hours; and
             reporting form, which is then used for national, State, and area               Form DL 1219—Monthly Report on Labor Turnover. These
             estimates. This eliminates duplicate reporting on the part of                  schedules are of the "shuttle" type, with space for each month
             respondents, and together with the use of identical techniques                 of the calendar year. The collection agency returns the schedule
             at the national and State levels, insures maximum comparability                to the respondent each month so that the next months data
             of estimates.                                                                  can be entered. This procedure assures maximum comparability

                                                                                  The link relative" technique
             Labor turnover
                                                                                     From a sample composed of establishments reporting for
                 Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary          both the previous and current months, the ratio of current
             workers into and out of employed status with respect to indi-        month employment to that of the previous month is computed.
             vidual establishments. This movement, which relates to a cal-        This is called a link relative. The estimates of employment (all
             endar month, is divided into two broad types: Accessions (new        employees, including production and nonproduction workers
             hires and rehires) and separations (terminations of employment       together) for the current month are obtained by multiplying the
             initiated by either employer or employee). Each type of action       estimates for the previous month by these "link relatives." In
             is cumulated for a calendar month and expressed as a rate per        addition, small bias correction factors are applied to selected
             100 employees. The data relate to all employees, whether full-       employment estimates each month. The size of the bias correc-
             or part-time, permanent or temporary, including executive,           tion factors is determined from past experience.Other features

             Relationship of labor turnover to employment series                      The estimates relating to the benchmark month are compared
                                                                                  with new benchmark levels, industry by industry. If revisions
                  Month-to-month changes in total employment in manufac-          are necessary, the monthly series of estimates are adjusted
              turing industries reflected by labor turnover rates are not com-    between the new benchmark and the preceding one, and the

                                                              Seasonal adjustment

                 Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring sea-      the appropriate series are aggregated. The unemployment rate
             sonal movement which can be estimated on the basis of past           for all civilian workers is derived by dividing the estimate for
             experience. By eliminating that part of the change which can         total unemployment (the sum of 4 seasonally adjusted sex-age
             be ascribed to usual seasonal variation, it is possible to observe   components) by the civilian labor force (the sum of 12 sea-
             the cyclical and other nonseasonal movements in the series.          sonally adjusted sex-age components).
              However, in evaluating deviations from the seasonal pattern —           Revised seasonally adjusted series for major components
             that is, changes in a seasonally adjusted series—it is important     of the labor force based on data through December 1976, new
             to note that seasonal adjustment is merely an approximation          seasonal factors for the 12 major components of the civilian
             based on past experience. Seasonally adjusted estimates have         labor force, and a description of the seasonal adjustment
             a broader margin of possible error than the original data on         methodology are published in the February 1977 Employment
             which they are based, since they are subject not only to sam-        and Earnings. Many additional series, which are either com-
             pling and other errors, but in addition, are affected by the un-     ponents or aggregates of the series presented, are available
             certainties of the seasonal adjustment process itself. Seasonally    from the BLS upon request.
